title: some/none: parser and compiler
analysis:
## Current State

CompiledExprKind::OptionSome(Box<CompiledExpr>) and CompiledExprKind::OptionNone already exist in reify-types/src/expr.rs (lines 86-90), including constructor methods (CompiledExpr::option_some, CompiledExpr::option_none), walk/traversal, collect_value_refs, remap_entity, and content hashing (tags [14]/[15]). Test support builders (option_some_expr, option_none_expr) also exist in reify-test-support.

Type::Option(Box<Type>) variant exists in reify-types/src/ty.rs.

However, the parser and compiler do NOT yet handle some(expr) or none expressions:
- some(expr) currently parses as ExprKind::FunctionCall { name: "some", args: [expr] } — correct syntax, no grammar changes needed.
- none currently parses as ExprKind::Ident("none") — correct syntax, no grammar changes needed.
- The compiler has NO special handling for either — some(expr) falls through to stdlib FunctionCall, none produces 'unresolved name' error.
- resolve_type_name() does NOT handle Option<T> parameterized types.

## Approach

No grammar or AST changes needed — some(expr) and none parse correctly via existing function_call and identifier rules.

**Parser work**: Write tests to verify some(expr) parses as FunctionCall and none parses as Ident. These should all pass immediately.

**Compiler work**:
1. Intercept ExprKind::Ident("none") before scope lookup in compile_expr_guarded → emit CompiledExprKind::OptionNone with default Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Real)).
2. Intercept ExprKind::FunctionCall { name: "some", .. } before function resolution → validate exactly 1 arg, compile it, emit CompiledExprKind::OptionSome(inner) with Type::Option(Box::new(inner.result_type)).
3. For none type inference from context: At param/let compilation sites, when default_expr is OptionNone and cell_type is Option<T>, override the OptionNone result_type.
4. Add Option<T> support to type resolution: When type_expr.name == "Option" and has 1 type_arg, resolve inner type and return Type::Option(Box::new(inner)).

## Key Code Locations
- compile_expr_guarded: lib.rs:880 (main expression compiler)
- Ident handling: lib.rs:920-948 (where none intercept goes)
- FunctionCall handling: lib.rs:1071-1175 (where some intercept goes)
- resolve_type_name: lib.rs:560-574 (needs Option<T> handling)
- Param type resolution: lib.rs:2644 (resolve_type_with_params call site)
- Param second pass: lib.rs:2795-2849 (type context for none)
- Let second pass: lib.rs:2850-2870 (type context for none)

prerequisites.description: Add Option<T> support to type resolution in reify-compiler. At param/let type resolution call sites (lib.rs ~2644 and similar), add check: if type_expr.name == 'Option' and type_expr.type_args.len() == 1, recursively resolve the inner type arg and return Type::Option(Box::new(inner)). This is a prerequisite because both some and none compilation tests need Option<T> type annotations to verify correct type inference.

steps.description: Write parser tests in crates/reify-syntax/tests/option_tests.rs: verify some(42) parses as FunctionCall{name:"some", args:[NumberLiteral(42)]}, some(x + 1) parses with BinOp arg, some(some(x)) nests correctly, none as let value parses as Ident("none"), none as param default parses as Ident("none"), some/none in conditional branches parse correctly. All tests should PASS immediately — no parser changes needed.

Write failing compiler test in crates/reify-compiler/tests/option_compile_tests.rs: compile 'structure S { let x = some(42) }' → expect CompiledExprKind::OptionSome with inner Literal(Int(42)) and result_type Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Int)). Currently fails because some(42) compiles as stdlib FunctionCall.

Implement some(expr) compilation in compile_expr_guarded's FunctionCall arm (lib.rs ~1071). Add early check BEFORE resolve_function_overload: if name == "some", validate exactly 1 arg (emit diagnostic if 0 or 2+), compile the single arg, return CompiledExpr::option_some(compiled_inner, Type::Option(Box::new(inner.result_type))).

Write failing compiler test: compile 'structure S { let x = none }' → expect CompiledExprKind::OptionNone with result_type Type::Option(_). Currently fails because none produces 'unresolved name' error.

Implement none compilation in compile_expr_guarded's Ident arm (lib.rs ~920). Add early check BEFORE scope.resolve: if name == "none", return CompiledExpr::option_none(Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Real))). Default inner type is Real; contextual override comes in step-7.

Write failing compiler test: compile 'structure S { param x: Option<Int> = none }' → expect OptionNone with result_type Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Int)). Currently fails because resolve_type_with_params doesn't handle Option<T> and none doesn't get type context from param annotation.

Implement Option<T> type resolution and none type context propagation. (a) At param type resolution (lib.rs ~2644), before calling resolve_type_with_params, add check: if type_expr.name == "Option" && type_expr.type_args.len() == 1, resolve the inner type arg recursively and return Type::Option(Box::new(inner)). (b) At param compilation (lib.rs ~2818), after compiling default_expr, if it's OptionNone and cell_type is Type::Option(inner), create new CompiledExpr::option_none with the correct Option type. (c) Same for let compilation (lib.rs ~2856): if compiled_expr is OptionNone and let_decl.type_expr resolves to Option<T>, override the result_type.

Write compiler tests for edge cases: (a) some() with 0 args → diagnostic error emitted, (b) some(1, 2) with 2 args → diagnostic error emitted, (c) nested some(some(42)) → OptionSome(OptionSome(Literal(42))) with type Option<Option<Int>>, (d) some(x) where x is a param → OptionSome(ValueRef) with Option<param_type>, (e) let with Option<Length> type annotation and none default → correct scalar type propagation.

Fix any edge case test failures from step-8. Expected: (a) Add arity validation with clear diagnostic messages for some() wrong arg count (e.g., 'some() requires exactly 1 argument, got N'). (b) Verify nested some works via recursive compilation. (c) Verify Option<Length> resolves to Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Scalar{dimension:LENGTH})) through type_args resolution.

Write compiler test for no-context none (none used without type annotation): 'structure S { let x = none }' → OptionNone with Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Real)) default, no error diagnostics. Verifies graceful fallback when type cannot be inferred.
Verify step-10 test passes (should work from step-5 implementation). If any adjustment needed for the no-annotation case, fix here.

design_decisions.decision: No grammar or AST changes — handle some/none as semantic keywords recognized in the compiler
design_decisions.rationale: some(expr) already parses naturally as FunctionCall and none as Ident via existing grammar rules. Adding grammar-level keywords would require tree-sitter regeneration and is unnecessary since the compiler can intercept these names before normal resolution. The task description says 'verify...parse correctly' confirming no parser changes are expected.

design_decisions.decision: Intercept some/none BEFORE normal function/identifier resolution in compile_expr_guarded
design_decisions.rationale: Placing the check early (before resolve_function_overload for some, before scope.resolve for none) prevents confusing error messages like 'unresolved name: none' or 'no matching overload for some'. These are language keywords, not user-defined functions or variables.

design_decisions.decision: Default none type is Type::Option(Box::new(Type::Real)), with contextual override at param/let compilation sites
design_decisions.rationale: compile_expr_guarded has no 'expected type' parameter, so we can't pass context down. Instead, we emit a default-typed OptionNone and fix it up at the call site where type annotations are available. This is minimally invasive — no signature changes to compile_expr or compile_expr_guarded. Type::Real default matches the project's existing fallback convention for unresolved types.

design_decisions.decision: Add Option<T> support via inline checks at type resolution call sites rather than modifying resolve_type_name signature
design_decisions.rationale: resolve_type_name takes only a &str name, not the full TypeExpr with type_args. Rather than changing its signature (which affects many call sites), we add checks at the specific param/let compilation points where the TypeExpr struct is available. This keeps changes localized to the task scope.

design_decisions.decision: Validate some() arity: exactly 1 argument required, emit diagnostic for wrong count
design_decisions.rationale: some(expr) semantically wraps exactly one value. Zero args is meaningless (not the same as none), and multiple args are ambiguous. A clear diagnostic helps users understand the API.

reuse.how: Already defined with full traversal support (walk, collect_value_refs, remap_entity, content_hash tags [14]/[15]). No changes needed in reify-types.
Use directly: option_some(inner, result_type) and option_none(result_type) to create compiled expressions.
Already exists in the type system. Create instances via Type::Option(Box::new(inner_type)).

reuse.what: Test helper builders: option_some_expr() and option_none_expr()
reuse.how: Available for constructing expected values in compiler test assertions.
No changes needed — body ref collection for purposes already has match arms for these variants.
Replicate the standard helper functions in option_tests.rs for parsing source and extracting declarations/members with error checking.
Replicate the standard helpers in option_compile_tests.rs for compiling source and extracting compiled expressions from value cells.
